How Do Minimum Wages Affect Workplace Injuries?", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["URL Source: https://research.upjohn.org/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1093&context=up_policybriefs Published Time: Thu, 19 Mar 2026 14:04:47 GMT Number of Pages: 5 Markdown Content: Upjohn Research Upjohn Institute Policy and Research Briefs Upjohn Res", "Jisung Park, and Anna Stansbury. 2026. \"How Do Minimum Wages Affect Workplace Injuries?\" Policy and Research Brief.", "Upjohn Institute for Employment Research. https://doi.org/10.17848/pb2026-94 This title is brought to you by the Upjohn Institute.", "These could include workplace amenities, scheduling, or health and safety.", "In the United States, about 2.5 million people are hurt on the job each year, and over 5,000 are killed."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: URL Source: https://research.upjohn.org/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1093&context=up_policybriefs Published Time: Thu, 19"}, {"url": "https://mitsloan.mit.edu/centers-initiatives/hsi/minimum-wage-increases-and-workplace-injuries-evidence-firm-responses-to-higher-labor-costs", "title": "Minimum Wage Increases and Workplace Injuries", "summary": "MIT Sloan Health Systems Initiative Research Minimum Wage Increases and Workplace Injuries: Evidence on Firm Responses to Higher Labor Costs Debates over minimum wage policy have historically centered on a relatively narrow set of outcomes, most notably employment effects and wage gains. This focus, while analytically expedient, risks obscuring a broader set of firm responses that operate through non-wage aspects of work. A recent study co-authored by Professor Anna Stansbury contributes to a growing literature that examines these non-wage margins.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Using administrative data on more than 13 million workers\u2019 compensation claims in California between 2000 and 2019, the authors document a consistent, quantitatively meaningful relationship: increases in the minimum wage are associated with higher wo", "A 10% increase in the minimum wage leads to approximately two to three additional injuries per 1,000 low-wage workers annually.", "Faced with higher wage bills, firms may economize on maintenance, training, or protective equipment.", "The authors estimate that the welfare cost of increased workplace injuries reduces the net benefit of higher wages by approximately 10% to 20%.", "If minimum wage increases generate incentives for work intensification, complementary policies, such as enhanced workplace safety enforcement or incentives for capital investment in safer production processes, may be necessary to preserve worker welf"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Using administrative data on more than 13 million workers\u2019 compensation claims in California between 2000 and 2019, the "}, {"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11803299/", "title": "The unintended effects of a large minimum wage increase on ...", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT", "perspectives": [], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": ""}, {"url": "https://ideas.repec.org/p/hcx/wpaper/2301.html", "title": "A higher minimum wage promotes workplace safety", "summary": "William Lester & Michael Reich, 2016. \"Minimum Wage Shocks, Employment Flows, and Labor Market Frictions,\" Journal of Labor Economics, University of Chicago Press, vol. 34(3), pages 663-704. - Dube, Arindrajit & Lester, T. The federal government through the Department of Labor has imposed a minimum wage since 1938. Nearly all the state governments also impose minimum wages.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The federal government through the Department of Labor has imposed a minimum wage since 1938.", "Background The federal minimum wage originated in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) signed by President Franklin Roosevelt on June 25, 1938.", "That wage is equivalent to $4.04 in today\u2019s purchasing power.", "The air transport industry was added in 1947, followed by retail trade in 1961.", "The construction industry, public schools, farms, laundries, and nursing homes were added in 1966, and coverage was extended to state and local government employees in 1974."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The federal government through the Department of Labor has imposed a minimum wage since 1938."}]}
